response_parsed.topic: How to manage and dealing with fake content on social media.
response_parsed.opinion:
As a software developer, I've seen firsthand how the digital world can be manipulated. Dealing with fake content isn't just about feeling frustrated—it's an actual problem that needs technical solutions too. When I'm scrolling through my feed in Rome during weekends, it often feels like half the posts are scams or misinformation disguised as helpful advice.

My low neuroticism keeps me calm most of the time, but seeing blatant lies spread so quickly still bothers me. It reminds me of how easily trust can be broken online; I remember my father teaching me about honesty in business when I was young, even if it's just family gatherings or helping with household repairs.

I've developed a system: first, check multiple sources before believing anything viral, then verify credentials—my master's degree helped here because I'm familiar with how information systems work. And honestly? If something doesn't sound right from my perspective as someone who understands algorithms and data flows, it probably isn't.
response_parsed.reasoning_summary: Based on my background in software development (conscientiousness helps analyze solutions), native-born Italian identity shaped by values of truthfulness from family education (lower/upper secondary levels), low neuroticism provides emotional stability when encountering fake content. I approach this as an engineer would—systematic verification using multiple sources and credential checks.
